title: How to Sign a PDF on Mac for Free (2026 Guide)
excerpt: Sign a PDF on Mac for free using Preview's trackpad, camera, or iPhone signature, or skip the apps and sign in your browser with no upload required.
content:
You need to send back a signed lease addendum by 5 PM, and the only computer near you is your MacBook. Maybe you don't have Adobe Acrobat installed. Maybe the document has six pages and you'd rather not print, sign, scan, and re-attach. Macs ship with everything you need to sign a PDF for free — and a free browser-based PDF signing tool can do the same job on every Mac, every macOS version, and on the iPad sitting next to you.

This guide shows three ways to sign a PDF on a Mac in 2026 — using Preview, using a browser tool that never uploads your file, and using an iPhone or iPad as a signing pad. It also covers when each method is the right call, how to lock the signed file so it can't be edited, and what U.S. law actually requires for an e-signature to hold up.

Why Macs Make PDF Signing Easy — But There's a Catch

Apple's Preview app has shipped with macOS since 2001 and has supported in-app PDF signatures for over a decade. You can capture a signature with the trackpad, hold a paper signature up to the FaceTime camera, or sign on a nearby iPhone or iPad and have the result appear in Preview seconds later. Saved signatures sync across your Mac fleet through iCloud Drive (Apple Support, "Fill out and sign PDF forms in Preview on Mac," 2025).

The catch: Preview's signature is a flat image annotation drawn on top of the page. It doesn't add a cryptographic seal, doesn't lock the file from further edits, and isn't tied to your identity in a way a court could automatically verify after the fact. For most everyday agreements that's fine — the signature is still legally enforceable under the federal ESIGN Act and state UETA equivalents. For a high-value contract or a regulated industry document, you may want a managed e-sign service or, at minimum, a separately encrypted, flattened final copy.

Three Ways to Sign a PDF on a Mac in 2026

You have three practical options. Each has a clear sweet spot:

  • Preview (built-in). Best when you're already in the Mac ecosystem, only sign occasionally, and want to use the trackpad, the camera, or a nearby iPhone.
  • Browser-based signer. Best when you also work on Windows or Linux, when you want zero-upload privacy without buying anything, or when you're using a friend's Mac and don't want your signature stored on it.
  • Adobe Acrobat or a managed e-sign service. Best when the recipient requires a certified signature format, when multiple parties need to sign in sequence, or when you need a full audit trail.

This guide focuses on the first two because they're free, fast, and cover the vast majority of real-world signing.

How to Sign a PDF in Preview

Preview is already installed on every Mac. Open the PDF in Preview — it's the default if you double-click — then click the Markup toolbar icon, the small toolbox-shaped button near the right of the title bar. The Markup bar slides down with annotation tools; one of them is a Sign icon shaped like a signature. The first time you click it, Preview asks how you want to capture your signature. After that, the saved signature is one click away.

Create a Signature with the Trackpad

This is the right method if you want a signature that looks consistent across every Mac you use, since it stores cleanly in iCloud.

  1. Click the Sign icon, then Create Signature, then Trackpad.
  2. Click the placeholder text in the dialog box.
  3. Sign your name on the trackpad with a finger. A capacitive stylus works on Force Touch trackpads but not on older models.
  4. Press any key on the keyboard to stop capturing.
  5. Click Done.

If you don't like how it came out, click Clear and try again. Most people land a clean signature on the third attempt.

Create a Signature with the Camera

Use this method if you want your signed PDF to look like a wet-ink signature on paper. It's a little more work to set up, but it produces the most natural-looking result.

  1. Sign your name on a clean white sheet of paper. Use a thick black pen — fine ballpoint lines disappear when scanned.
  2. Click the Sign icon, then Create Signature, then Camera.
  3. Hold the paper up to the FaceTime camera so your signature sits roughly along the blue baseline shown on screen.
  4. Wait two or three seconds for Preview to capture it.
  5. Click Done.

Preview removes the paper background on-device, leaving just the signature strokes. Lighting matters: face a window or a desk lamp rather than the ceiling, and avoid direct sun, which causes glare.

Create a Signature with iPhone or iPad

This is the cleanest option if you own an Apple Pencil. The signature ends up sharper than either the trackpad or the camera, and the iPad's fixed surface makes it easy to anchor a steady line.

  1. Click the Sign icon, then Create Signature, then Select Device, and pick your iPhone or iPad.
  2. The device wakes up and displays a signing surface.
  3. Sign with a finger or Apple Pencil.
  4. Tap Done on the device.

Both devices need to be signed into the same Apple ID with Bluetooth and Wi-Fi on. On iPad, the Apple Pencil's pressure sensitivity translates into stroke thickness, which is what makes this method look the closest to a real pen on paper.

Drop the Signature onto the PDF

Once a signature is saved, drop it onto the page:

  1. Click the Sign icon and pick the signature thumbnail.
  2. The signature appears as a draggable, resizable object on the page.
  3. Move it onto the signature line, then drag a corner handle to resize.
  4. Choose File → Save (Cmd+S) to write the change to the document.

Preview saves the signature as a flat annotation inside the PDF. To stop anyone from moving or replacing it, see the flattening section below.

How to Sign a PDF in Your Browser (No Apps, No Account)

Preview is convenient, but a browser-based signer is the right tool when you need maximum privacy, when you're on someone else's Mac, or when you also use Windows and Linux machines and want one consistent workflow.

  1. Open Sign PDF in Safari, Chrome, Firefox, or Edge.
  2. Drag your PDF onto the page or click to choose it from Finder.
  3. Either type your name in a script font or draw your signature with the trackpad (or with an Apple Pencil on iPad).
  4. Drag the signature onto the right spot, resize it, and add a date if needed.
  5. Click Download to save the signed PDF.

The whole process happens in your browser tab. The PDF is parsed, edited, and re-saved using JavaScript and WebAssembly libraries — there is no upload to any server, no account creation, and no daily cap. You can verify this in Safari's Web Inspector under the Network tab: clear it before signing, then run the flow, and you should see no outbound requests carrying file data during processing.

The browser approach is also macOS-version-proof. Older Macs running Catalina or Big Sur sometimes fail to capture trackpad signatures cleanly, and Preview's signature feature has been known to silently break for some users after major macOS updates. A browser tool sidesteps the problem entirely and runs the same way on a 2014 MacBook Air as on a 2025 Mac Studio.

Mac Signing Methods Compared

Are E-Signatures on a Mac Legally Binding?

Yes, with caveats. The federal Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (ESIGN, 2000) and the state-level Uniform Electronic Transactions Act (UETA) together make most electronic signatures as enforceable as wet-ink ones across the United States. UETA has been adopted by 49 states, the District of Columbia, and several U.S. territories (Wikipedia, "Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act," 2025).

For a signature to hold up in court, four things generally need to be true (NCUA, "Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (E-Sign Act)," 2024):

  • Intent to sign. The signer knew they were entering a contract.
  • Consent to do business electronically. Both parties agreed to use electronic records.
  • Association with the record. The signature is tied to the document being signed.
  • Record retention. A copy of the signed record is kept and is reproducible later.

ESIGN does not cover a few document types. Wills, codicils, testamentary trusts, divorce decrees, adoption papers, and matters governed by the Uniform Commercial Code generally still require ink (Adobe, "Electronic Signature Laws and Regulations — United States," 2025). For high-stakes business contracts, a managed e-signature platform that captures IP address, timestamp, and an audit trail is a stronger evidentiary record than a Preview annotation, even though both are legally valid.

Flatten the Signed PDF So It Can't Be Edited

The signature you drop in Preview is technically a separate annotation layer. Anyone who opens the file in another tool can move it, delete it, or replace it. To lock it down, flatten the PDF — merging all annotations into the page itself.

The simplest path on a Mac:

  1. Open the signed PDF in Flatten PDF in your browser.
  2. Drop the file in.
  3. Download the flattened version.

The flattened file looks identical, but the signature is now part of the page bitmap rather than a movable object. For more on why flattening matters and which annotations get baked in, see our flattened PDF explainer.

If the recipient also needs to be sure the file wasn't modified after you signed it, add a password with Protect PDF after flattening. The combination — flatten, then encrypt — produces a tamper-evident, recipient-restricted document without any specialized software.

Common Mac Signing Problems and How to Fix Them

A handful of issues come up repeatedly. Here's how to deal with them:

  • The Sign icon is missing from the Markup toolbar. You're probably looking at a non-PDF file (an image or screenshot). Preview shows different annotation tools depending on the file type. Confirm the file extension is .pdf.
  • Trackpad signature comes out tiny or jagged. The trackpad capture window has a fixed size; sign large and slow. If your trackpad is grimy, wipe it — Preview misreads contact points on greasy surfaces.
  • Camera signature picks up paper edges. Use plain white printer paper, not lined notebook paper. Hold the paper steady, lit from the front but not under direct sunlight, which causes glare.
  • iPhone signature won't sync. Both devices need to be on the same Apple ID with Bluetooth and Wi-Fi on. Toggle Continuity off and on under System Settings → General → AirDrop & Handoff.
  • Saved signatures disappear between sessions. Make sure iCloud Drive is enabled under System Settings → Apple ID → iCloud. Without it, signatures live only on the Mac that captured them.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I sign a PDF on a Mac without installing any apps?

Yes. Preview is already installed on every Mac, and a free browser-based signer works in Safari without any download. The browser approach also runs on someone else's Mac without leaving any trace — your signature is never stored on that machine.

Is Preview's signature feature legally binding?

For most documents, yes. The signature satisfies ESIGN's requirement of an "electronic sound, symbol, or process" attached to or logically associated with a record. For very high-value contracts or regulated transactions, a service that captures audit-trail metadata such as DocuSign or Adobe Sign provides stronger evidence in a dispute.

Can I sign a PDF on a Mac with no trackpad?

Yes. Use the camera method to capture a wet-ink signature from paper, or use the iPhone or iPad method if you have a nearby device on the same Apple ID. Both work on Mac mini and Mac Pro setups that don't include a trackpad.

How do I edit a signature I've already saved in Preview?

You can't edit an existing signature, only delete it and create a new one. Click the Sign icon, hover over the signature thumbnail, and click the small "x" that appears. Then create a fresh signature using whichever method you prefer.

Will the signature look the same when the recipient opens it?

If you flattened the PDF, yes — the signature is part of the page bitmap and renders identically in Preview, Acrobat, browsers, and mobile readers. If you didn't flatten it, the position is preserved but the layered annotation may render slightly differently in non-Apple PDF viewers.

Can I use Apple Pencil with my Mac for signatures?

Indirectly. The Mac has no native Apple Pencil input. Use the iPhone or iPad signing method described above — sign on the iPad with the Pencil, and the signature transfers to the Mac in seconds. The result is sharper than trackpad capture on every MacBook model.

Does Preview work on PDF forms with fillable fields?

Yes. Click any field and start typing. Starting in macOS Sonoma, Preview detects form-enabled PDFs and shows an AutoFill Form button in the top-right corner; clicking it fills in fields it can match from your Contacts card. macOS 14.2 and later support enhanced AutoFill for names, addresses, and phone numbers.

Sign Your Next PDF on a Mac in Under a Minute

Whether you reach for Preview or a free browser signer, signing a PDF on a Mac in 2026 takes well under a minute and never needs to upload your file to a third-party server. For the occasional signature, Preview's trackpad or camera capture is the path of least resistance. For cross-platform consistency, an iPad-Pencil-perfect signature, or maximum privacy when you don't fully trust the machine you're on, the browser-based signer is the better fit.

After signing, take fifteen extra seconds to flatten the file so the signature can't be moved or replaced. That single step is the difference between a casual signed copy and a tamper-evident one.

For other workflows, see our guides to signing PDFs online from any operating system and signing PDFs directly on an iPhone.

This article is for general informational purposes only. PDF behavior can vary between viewers, operating systems, and PDF versions. Tiny PDF Tools processes your files entirely in your browser — nothing is uploaded to our servers.
